---
title: "quarto-dbml Example"
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
filters:
  - quarto-dbml
dbml:
  theme: light
---

## E-Commerce Schema

A multi-schema DBML diagram demonstrating tables, enums, indexes, notes,
inline refs, named refs, and cross-schema relationships.

```{.dbml theme="light"}
// ── Enums ────────────────────────────────────────────────
Enum order_status {
  pending
  processing
  shipped
  delivered
  cancelled [note: 'Soft delete — row is retained']
}

Enum product_status {
  active
  discontinued
  out_of_stock
}

// ── public schema ─────────────────────────────────────────
Table public.users {
  id        integer     [pk, increment]
  email     varchar     [not null, unique]
  full_name varchar
  role      varchar     [default: 'customer']
  created_at timestamp  [not null]

  Note: 'Registered application users'

  Indexes {
    email       [unique, name: 'uq_users_email']
    created_at  [name: 'idx_users_created']
  }
}

Table public.addresses {
  id         integer  [pk, increment]
  user_id    integer  [not null]
  line1      varchar  [not null]
  line2      varchar
  city       varchar  [not null]
  country    varchar(2) [not null]
  is_default boolean  [default: false]
}

// ── ecommerce schema ──────────────────────────────────────
Table ecommerce.products {
  id          integer               [pk, increment]
  name        varchar               [not null]
  description text
  price       decimal(10,2)         [not null]
  status      product_status        [default: 'active']
  category_id integer
  created_at  timestamp             [default: `now()`]

  Indexes {
    (status, category_id)  [name: 'idx_products_status_cat']
    name                   [name: 'idx_products_name']
  }
}

Table ecommerce.categories {
  id        integer  [pk, increment]
  name      varchar  [not null, unique]
  parent_id integer

  Note: 'Self-referencing for nested categories'
}

Table ecommerce.orders {
  id          integer       [pk, increment]
  user_id     integer       [not null]
  address_id  integer       [not null]
  status      order_status  [default: 'pending']
  total       decimal(10,2) [not null]
  note        text
  created_at  timestamp     [default: `now()`]

  Indexes {
    user_id    [name: 'idx_orders_user']
    status     [name: 'idx_orders_status']
  }
}

Table ecommerce.order_items {
  id         integer       [pk, increment]
  order_id   integer       [not null]
  product_id integer       [not null]
  quantity   integer       [not null]
  unit_price decimal(10,2) [not null]

  Indexes {
    (order_id, product_id) [unique, name: 'uq_order_items']
  }
}

// ── Relationships ─────────────────────────────────────────
Ref: public.addresses.user_id > public.users.id
Ref: ecommerce.orders.user_id > public.users.id
Ref: ecommerce.orders.address_id > public.addresses.id
Ref: ecommerce.order_items.order_id > ecommerce.orders.id
Ref: ecommerce.order_items.product_id > ecommerce.products.id
Ref: ecommerce.products.category_id > ecommerce.categories.id
Ref cat_parent: ecommerce.categories.parent_id > ecommerce.categories.id
```

## Orthogonal routing (sharp corners)

```{.dbml routing="orthogonal"}
Table users {
  id    integer [pk, increment]
  email varchar [not null, unique]
}

Table posts {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  user_id integer [not null]
  title   varchar [not null]
}

Table comments {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  post_id integer [not null]
  user_id integer [not null]
  body    text    [not null]
}

Ref: posts.user_id    > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id > users.id
```

## Rounded routing (90° bends with rounded corners)

```{.dbml routing="rounded"}
Table users {
  id    integer [pk, increment]
  email varchar [not null, unique]
}

Table posts {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  user_id integer [not null]
  title   varchar [not null]
}

Table comments {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  post_id integer [not null]
  user_id integer [not null]
  body    text    [not null]
}

Ref: posts.user_id    > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id > users.id
```

## UML notation

```{.dbml notation="uml"}
Table users {
  id    integer [pk, increment]
  email varchar [not null, unique]
}

Table posts {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  user_id integer [not null]
  title   varchar [not null]
}

Table comments {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  post_id integer [not null]
  user_id integer [not null]
  body    text    [not null]
}

Ref: posts.user_id    > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id > users.id
```

## Arrow notation

```{.dbml notation="arrows"}
Table users {
  id    integer [pk, increment]
  email varchar [not null, unique]
}

Table posts {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  user_id integer [not null]
  title   varchar [not null]
}

Table comments {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  post_id integer [not null]
  user_id integer [not null]
  body    text    [not null]
}

Ref: posts.user_id    > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id > users.id
```

## Crow's foot notation

```{.dbml notation="crowsfoot"}
Table users {
  id    integer [pk, increment]
  email varchar [not null, unique]
}

Table posts {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  user_id integer [not null]
  title   varchar [not null]
}

Table comments {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  post_id integer [not null]
  user_id integer [not null]
  body    text    [not null]
}

Ref: posts.user_id    > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id > users.id
```

## Echo: show DBML source alongside the diagram

```{.dbml echo="true"}
Table users {
  id    integer [pk, increment]
  email varchar [not null, unique]
}

Table posts {
  id      integer [pk, increment]
  user_id integer [not null]
  title   varchar [not null]
}

Ref: posts.user_id > users.id
```

## Detail levels (interactive)

Use the button in the top-left corner of each diagram to cycle between detail
levels: **All** (all columns), **Key** (PK + FK columns only), **Hdr** (table
names only).

The `level=` attribute sets the *initial* level. Users can still toggle in the
browser.

```{.dbml level="full"}
Table users {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  email       varchar   [not null, unique]
  full_name   varchar
  role        varchar   [default: 'customer']
  created_at  timestamp [not null]
}

Table posts {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  user_id     integer   [not null]
  title       varchar   [not null]
  body        text
  published   boolean   [default: false]
  created_at  timestamp [default: `now()`]
}

Table comments {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  post_id     integer   [not null]
  user_id     integer   [not null]
  body        text      [not null]
  created_at  timestamp [default: `now()`]
}

Ref: posts.user_id    > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id > users.id
```

## Keys-only initial view

```{.dbml level="keys"}
Table users {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  email       varchar   [not null, unique]
  full_name   varchar
  role        varchar   [default: 'customer']
  created_at  timestamp [not null]
}

Table posts {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  user_id     integer   [not null]
  title       varchar   [not null]
  body        text
  published   boolean   [default: false]
  created_at  timestamp [default: `now()`]
}

Table comments {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  post_id     integer   [not null]
  user_id     integer   [not null]
  body        text      [not null]
  created_at  timestamp [default: `now()`]
}

Ref: posts.user_id    > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id > users.id
```

## Names-only initial view (large schemas at a glance)

```{.dbml level="names"}
Table users {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  email       varchar   [not null, unique]
  full_name   varchar
  role        varchar
  created_at  timestamp
}

Table posts {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  user_id     integer   [not null]
  title       varchar
  body        text
  published   boolean
}

Table comments {
  id          integer   [pk, increment]
  post_id     integer   [not null]
  user_id     integer   [not null]
  body        text
}

Table tags {
  id    integer [pk, increment]
  name  varchar [not null, unique]
}

Table post_tags {
  post_id integer [not null]
  tag_id  integer [not null]
}

Ref: posts.user_id      > users.id
Ref: comments.post_id   > posts.id
Ref: comments.user_id   > users.id
Ref: post_tags.post_id  > posts.id
Ref: post_tags.tag_id   > tags.id
```

## Auto-arrange layouts

Use the **Grd** button (third from top-left) to cycle through layout modes:
**Grid → LR → TB → Ctr → Grid**.

- **Grd** — original grid (default)
- **LR** — left-right hierarchical: dimension/master tables on the left, fact/transaction tables on the right
- **TB** — top-bottom hierarchical: same logic, vertically
- **Ctr** — radial: most-connected table at center, others in concentric rings

### Single-fact data warehouse (star/snowflake schema)

Fact table has four direct dimension relationships; two dimensions share a
sub-dimension (`dim_region`), making this a snowflake. **Ctr** mode should
place `fact_orders` at the center with dimension tables ringing it.

```{.dbml}
Table fact_orders {
  order_key    integer      [pk]
  date_key     integer      [not null]
  customer_key integer      [not null]
  product_key  integer      [not null]
  store_key    integer      [not null]
  quantity     integer      [not null]
  revenue      decimal(12,2)
  discount     decimal(5,2)
}

Table dim_date {
  date_key    integer [pk]
  full_date   date
  year        integer
  quarter     integer
  month       integer
  day_of_week varchar
}

Table dim_product {
  product_key integer      [pk]
  name        varchar      [not null]
  sku         varchar      [unique]
  category    varchar
  brand       varchar
  unit_cost   decimal(10,2)
}

Table dim_customer {
  customer_key integer [pk]
  name         varchar [not null]
  email        varchar
  segment      varchar
  region_key   integer [not null]
}

Table dim_store {
  store_key  integer [pk]
  name       varchar [not null]
  city       varchar
  region_key integer [not null]
}

Table dim_region {
  region_key integer [pk]
  name       varchar [not null]
  country    varchar [not null]
}

Ref: fact_orders.date_key     > dim_date.date_key
Ref: fact_orders.customer_key > dim_customer.customer_key
Ref: fact_orders.product_key  > dim_product.product_key
Ref: fact_orders.store_key    > dim_store.store_key
Ref: dim_customer.region_key  > dim_region.region_key
Ref: dim_store.region_key     > dim_region.region_key
```

### Multi-fact data warehouse with shared dimensions

Two fact tables (`fact_sales`, `fact_inventory`) share dimension tables.
`dim_region` is a sub-dimension (snowflake arm). `config_thresholds` is
entirely unrelated — in **LR** mode it should land at the far right; in
**Ctr** mode it should appear in the outermost ring.

```{.dbml}
Table fact_sales {
  sale_key     integer      [pk]
  date_key     integer      [not null]
  product_key  integer      [not null]
  customer_key integer      [not null]
  store_key    integer      [not null]
  quantity     integer
  revenue      decimal(12,2)
}

Table fact_inventory {
  snapshot_key integer [pk]
  date_key     integer [not null]
  product_key  integer [not null]
  store_key    integer [not null]
  units_on_hand integer
  units_ordered integer
}

Table dim_date {
  date_key  integer [pk]
  full_date date
  year      integer
  quarter   integer
  month     integer
}

Table dim_product {
  product_key integer      [pk]
  name        varchar
  category    varchar
  unit_cost   decimal(10,2)
}

Table dim_customer {
  customer_key integer [pk]
  name         varchar
  segment      varchar
  region_key   integer [not null]
}

Table dim_store {
  store_key  integer [pk]
  name       varchar
  region_key integer [not null]
}

Table dim_region {
  region_key integer [pk]
  name       varchar
  country    varchar
}

Table config_thresholds {
  id          integer [pk, increment]
  metric_name varchar [not null, unique]
  warn_value  decimal(12,2)
  crit_value  decimal(12,2)
  updated_at  timestamp
}

Ref: fact_sales.date_key        > dim_date.date_key
Ref: fact_sales.product_key     > dim_product.product_key
Ref: fact_sales.customer_key    > dim_customer.customer_key
Ref: fact_sales.store_key       > dim_store.store_key
Ref: fact_inventory.date_key    > dim_date.date_key
Ref: fact_inventory.product_key > dim_product.product_key
Ref: fact_inventory.store_key   > dim_store.store_key
Ref: dim_customer.region_key    > dim_region.region_key
Ref: dim_store.region_key       > dim_region.region_key
```

## Block-level theme override (forced dark)

```{.dbml theme="dark"}
Table sessions {
  id         varchar    [pk, note: 'UUID']
  user_id    integer    [not null]
  ip_address varchar
  user_agent text
  expires_at timestamp  [not null]
  created_at timestamp  [default: `now()`]

  Indexes {
    user_id    [name: 'idx_sessions_user']
    expires_at [name: 'idx_sessions_expiry']
  }
}

Table public.users {
  id    integer  [pk, increment]
  email varchar  [not null, unique]
}

Ref: sessions.user_id > public.users.id
```
